card.intuition: Greedily settle the closest unsettled vertex at each step. Use a min-heap to always pick the minimum distance vertex efficiently. Only works with non-negative edge weights — a negative edge could make a 'settled' vertex cheaper via a later path.
card.analogy: Finding the fastest route on a road map by expanding outward like a ripple from the source — always following the next closest city. Once a city is reached, its shortest route is final.
card.time_complexity: O((V + E) log V) with a binary heap
when_to_use: Single-source shortest paths on graphs with non-negative edge weights
As a subroutine in A*, Johnson's algorithm, and MST algorithms
gotchas: Does NOT work with negative weights — use Bellman-Ford instead
Lazy deletion: push (new_dist, node) to heap; skip when popped if dist[node] is already better
For dense graphs, an adjacency matrix + linear scan (O(V²)) can beat the heap approach

exercise.prompt: Implement Dijkstra's algorithm returning shortest distances from start to all vertices.
hints: Only relax an edge (u→v) when d + w < dist[v] — if the new distance is better, update dist[v] and push the new entry onto the heap
